content:
# Leviticus Chapter 24 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 72v–73r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_024_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_024_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 110–111 | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_024_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_024_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on structure: Leviticus 24 is a structurally unusual chapter in the Holiness Code, combining ritual prescriptions for two permanent sanctuary objects — the menorah-lamp (vv.1–4) and the lechem ha-panim, the "bread of the Presence/Face" (vv.5–9) — with a narrative episode of explosive judicial significance: the case of the meqallel, the blasphemer (vv.10–23). The chapter thus mirrors the pattern found elsewhere in Leviticus where narrative interrupts law (cf. Lev 10, Lev 16), and the narrative itself generates law. The blasphemer is the son of an Israelite woman (Shelomit bat Divri, of the tribe of Dan — the only named laywoman in Leviticus) and an Egyptian man; his ambiguous status as a "mixed" person (ben ha-yisre'elit) is likely relevant to the question of whether the laws of the Name apply to him. The divine verdict is unambiguous: one law (mishpat echad) for the sojourner and the native. The lex talionis passage (vv.17–22) embedded within the narrative is remarkable: it is not presented as new law but as the explanatory framework that makes the ruling on the blasphemer intelligible — life answers for life, measure for measure. Three key terms drive the theological argument: (a) va-yiqqov — "he pierced/specified/blasphemed the Name" (v.11, 16) — the verb n-q-v, which in this context means to "pierce" or "utter distinctly" the divine Name in a context of cursing, a grave violation of the sanctity of the Name (cf. Exod 20:7); (b) ve-qillel — "and he cursed" (v.11, 15) — the Pi'el of q-l-l, "to curse/make light of/treat as contemptible," the opposite of kabod (honor/weight); and (c) mishpat echad — "one law" (v.22) — the chapter's climactic theological assertion that the covenant-community's legal system applies without distinction of origin. The ritual legislation (vv.1–9) anchors the chapter in the permanent, ordered sanctuary life — a contrast to the disorder of the blasphemy narrative that follows.]*

---

## Leviticus 24

**24:1** And the LORD spoke to Moshe, saying:

**24:2** Command [tsav — צַו; the Piel imperative of ts-w-h, "to command"; the verb used for direct, authoritative ordering — the same root as mitsvah; unlike "speak" (daber) or "say" (emor), tsav carries the force of an ongoing, binding charge] the sons of Yisra'el [et-benei Yisra'el — אֶת-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל], and they shall take [ve-yiqchu — וְיִקְחוּ; the plural imperfect with vav-consecutive — "and they shall take/bring"; the command is addressed collectively to the community to supply the oil] to you [elekha — אֵלֶיךָ; to Moshe, who transmits it to Aharon] pure beaten olive oil [shemen zayit zakh katit — שֶׁמֶן זַיִת זַךְ כָּתִית; shemen — "oil"; zayit — "olive"; zakh — "pure/clear/bright," from z-k-k, "to be clear/translucent"; katit — "beaten," from k-t-t, "to beat/pound"; the oil is produced not by pressing in a press but by beating/pounding the olives so that only the first pure flow is collected, without sediment; the combination zakh katit — "pure beaten" — specifies the highest quality of olive oil] for the light [la-ma'or — לַמָּאוֹר; ma'or — "luminary/lamp/light-source," from '-w-r, "to be light"; the ma'or is the lamp-installation as a whole; distinct from ner, the individual lamp-flame], to cause a lamp to go up [le-ha'alot ner — לְהַעֲלוֹת נֵר; the Hifil infinitive of '-l-h, "to cause to ascend/go up"; the standard idiom for lighting a lamp in the sanctuary — the flame "goes up" or is "raised up"; not merely "to light" but to make the light ascend; ner — the individual flame or lamp] continually [tamid — תָּמִיד; "always/perpetually/regularly"; the word does not necessarily mean without ceasing through the night, but rather as a standing permanent institution — the lamp is never permanently extinguished].

**24:3** Outside the veil of the testimony [mi-chutz la-parokhet ha-edut — מִחוּץ לַפָּרֹכֶת הָעֵדֻת; the parokhet — the inner curtain/veil of the tabernacle that separates the Holy Place from the Holy of Holies (Exod 26:31–33); ha-edut — "of the testimony," referring to the aron ha-edut, the ark of the testimony (Exod 25:22), the gold-overlaid chest containing the tablets of the covenant; the lamp stands in the Holy Place, outside — though facing — the inner veil], in the Tent of Meeting [be-ohel mo'ed — בְּאֹהֶל מוֹעֵד; the Tabernacle as the appointed place of divine-human meeting], Aharon shall arrange it [ya'arokh oto Aharon — יַעֲרֹךְ אֹתוֹ אַהֲרֹן; ya'arokh — Qal imperfect of '-r-k, "to arrange/set in order"; the verb used for setting the battle-line and for arranging items in proper order; here it denotes the priest's careful setting of the lamps] from evening to morning [me-erev ad-boqer — מֵעֶרֶב עַד-בֹּקֶר; the lamp burns through the night — from evening's onset to the morning] before the LORD [lifnei YHWH — לִפְנֵי יְהוָה; in the presence of, in front of, the LORD — the lamp burns as an act of service before the divine Presence] — a statute forever [chuqqat olam — חֻקַּת עוֹלָם; a permanent, binding ordinance for all generations] throughout your generations [le-doroteikem — לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם].

**24:4** On the pure lampstand [al-ha-menorah ha-tehorah — עַל-הַמְּנֹרָה הַטְּהֹרָה; the menorah — the seven-branched golden lampstand (Exod 25:31–40); ha-tehorah — "the pure/clean one," designating its ritual purity and its material of pure gold (zahav tahor); the epithet distinguishes this lampstand from any other and marks its sacred status] he shall arrange [ya'arokh — יַעֲרֹךְ; repeated from v.3; the priest's task is ongoing, rhythmic ordering] the lamps [et-ha-nerot — אֶת-הַנֵּרוֹת; nerot — plural of ner; the seven individual lamp-cups of the menorah] before the LORD [lifnei YHWH — לִפְנֵי יְהוָה] continually [tamid — תָּמִיד].

**24:5** And you shall take [ve-laqachta — וְלָקַחְתָּ; singular — addressed to Moshe, or more broadly to the one responsible for the preparation] fine flour [solet — סֹלֶת; the finest sifted wheat flour, as in the grain offerings; the same quality used throughout the Levitical offerings] and you shall bake [ve-afita — וְאָפִיתָ; the Qal perfect of '-p-h, "to bake"] it into twelve loaves [otah sheteim esreh challot — אֹתָהּ שְׁתֵּים עֶשְׂרֵה חַלּוֹת; challot — plural of challah, from ch-l-l, "to bore/pierce" or related to ch-l-h, used for bread perforated or pierced; a round or flat loaf; twelve — corresponding to the twelve tribes of Yisra'el]; two-tenths [shnei esronim — שְׁנֵי עֶשְׂרֹנִים; esron — one-tenth of an ephah (approximately two liters); two-tenths per loaf; twelve loaves at two-tenths each equals twenty-four-tenths of an ephah of flour for the full set] shall be in the one loaf [yihyeh ha-challah ha-echat — יִהְיֶה הַחַלָּה הָאֶחָת; each individual loaf receives two-tenths of fine flour, making them substantial, uniform, and equal].

**24:6** And you shall place them [ve-samta otam — וְשַׂמְתָּ אוֹתָם; from s-w-m/s-y-m, "to place/set/put"; the precise arrangement is specified in what follows] in two rows [shetei ma'arakbot — שְׁתֵּי מַעֲרָכוֹת; ma'arakot — plural of ma'arakah, from '-r-k, the same root as ya'arokh in vv.3–4; "arrangements/rows/battle-lines"; two rows of six loaves each], six to a row [sheshet ha-ma'arakah — שִׁשָּׁה הַמַּעֲרָכָה; lit. "six the row" — six per arrangement], upon the pure table [al-ha-shulchan ha-tahor — עַל-הַשֻּׁלְחָן הַטָּהוֹר; the shulchan — the Table of the Presence, the gold-overlaid acacia-wood table inside the Holy Place (Exod 25:23–30); ha-tahor — "the pure one," as with the menorah; both sanctuary furnishings carry this epithet marking their consecrated purity] before the LORD [lifnei YHWH — לִפְנֵי יְהוָה].

**24:7** And you shall place [ve-natata — וְנָתַתָּ; from n-t-n, "to give/place/put"; different verb from samta in v.6, but used interchangeably here for the action of setting the incense] upon each row [al-ha-ma'arakah — עַל-הַמַּעֲרָכָה; "upon the row" — the preposition implies placement on or alongside each row] pure frankincense [levonah zakah — לְבֹנָה זַכָּה; levonah — frankincense, a resinous incense of great value and aroma; zakah — "pure/clean," the feminine form of zakh (cf. v.2); the same quality designation used for the oil; pure uncompounded frankincense], and it shall be for the bread [ve-hayetah la-lechem — וְהָיְתָה לַלֶּחֶם; the frankincense "belongs to" or "serves as a memorial for" the bread] as a memorial portion [le-azkarah — לְאַזְכָּרָה; azkarah — "reminder/memorial portion," from z-k-r, "to remember"; the portion of an offering that is burned on the altar as a "reminder" before the LORD, causing the LORD to "remember" or acknowledge the offering; in grain offerings the azkarah is the handful burned on the altar (Lev 2:2, 9, 16); here the frankincense serves that function for the bread, since the loaves themselves are not burned], a fire-offering to the LORD [isheh la-YHWH — אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה; the frankincense is what is actually burned — it is the fire-offering constituent of this complex ritual].

**24:8** On every sabbath day [be-yom ha-shabbat be-yom ha-shabbat — בְּיוֹם הַשַּׁבָּת בְּיוֹם הַשַּׁבָּת; the doubling of the phrase "on the sabbath day, on the sabbath day" is emphatic — "every single sabbath"; a Hebrew idiom of reiteration expressing regularity and certainty] he shall arrange it [ya'arkhenu — יַעַרְכֶנּוּ; the same verb '-r-k as throughout vv.3–6; the priest arranges the fresh bread each sabbath] before the LORD [lifnei YHWH — לִפְנֵי יְהוָה] continually [tamid — תָּמִיד; the same tamid as in vv.2–4 — the arrangement is a permanent, unbroken institution], from the sons of Yisra'el [me-et benei Yisra'el — מֵאֵת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל; the bread is provided by the community collectively — it is their ongoing covenantal gift] — an everlasting covenant [berit olam — בְּרִית עוֹלָם; the bread of the Presence is designated specifically as berit olam — "a covenant of eternity/an everlasting covenant"; this is a striking designation: the regular replacement of the bread encodes the idea of a permanent, self-renewing covenant bond between the LORD and Yisra'el].

**24:9** And it shall belong to Aharon and to his sons [ve-hayetah le-Aharon u-le-vanav — וְהָיְתָה לְאַהֲרֹן וּלְבָנָיו; the priestly prerogative — the bread of the Presence is eaten by the priests, not burned on the altar beyond its frankincense], and they shall eat it [va-akhaluhu — וַאֲכָלֻהוּ; Qal perfect with vav-consecutive — "and they shall eat it"] in a holy place [be-maqom qadosh — בְּמָקוֹם קָדוֹשׁ]; for it is most holy for him [ki qodesh qodashim hu lo — כִּי קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים הוּא לוֹ; qodesh qodashim — "holiness of holinesses/most holy"; the superlative construction in Hebrew uses this repetition; the bread is classified at the highest level of holiness — like the sin offering and guilt offering (Lev 6–7)] from the fire-offerings of the LORD [me-ishei YHWH — מֵאִשֵּׁי יְהוָה; the bread is technically part of the fire-offering complex by virtue of the frankincense burned with it; the priests receive their portion "from the fire-offerings" — a share in what is presented to the LORD], a statute forever [chuqqat olam — חֻקַּת עוֹלָם].

---

**24:10** And the son of a woman of Yisra'el went out [va-yetse ben-ishah Yisra'elit — וַיֵּצֵא בֶּן-אִשָּׁה יִשְׂרְאֵלִית; the narrative begins with va-yetse — "and he went out" — an abrupt introduction of a new character; "went out" suggests he left somewhere — perhaps his tent, or the camp's interior; the character is defined primarily by maternal lineage — "son of a woman of Yisra'el"], and he was the son of an Egyptian man [ve-hu ben-ish Mitsri — וְהוּא בֶן-אִישׁ מִצְרִי; his paternal lineage is Egyptian — his father was Mitsri, an Egyptian; this dual parentage creates his ambiguous legal status: is he a full member of the covenant community or a ger, a sojourner?], among the sons of Yisra'el [be-tokh benei Yisra'el — בְּתוֹךְ בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל; he dwells within the community of Yisra'el — he is not a stranger from outside]; and the son of the woman of Yisra'el [va-yinnatsev ben-ha-Yisra'elit — וַיִּנָּצֵב בֶּן-הַיִּשְׂרְאֵלִית; the Niphal of n-ts-b, "to station oneself/to stand/to take a position"; implies a confrontational or deliberate positioning] and a man of Yisra'el [ve-ish ha-Yisra'eli — וְאִישׁ הַיִּשְׂרְאֵלִי; the man with whom he quarrels is a full native Israelite — the contrast with the mixed-parentage man is deliberate] strove together [va-yinnatsu be-tokh ha-machaneh — וַיִּנָּצוּ בְּתוֹךְ הַמַּחֲנֶה; the Niphal of n-ts-h, "to quarrel/strive/fight"; the scene: a fight breaks out within the camp itself — the holy camp whose holiness is the subject of so much of Leviticus].

**24:11** And the son of the woman of Yisra'el blasphemed the Name [va-yiqqov ben-ha-Yisra'elit et-ha-Shem — וַיִּקֹּב בֶּן-הַיִּשְׂרְאֵלִית אֶת-הַשֵּׁם; va-yiqqov — the Qal imperfect of n-q-v, a verb with a range of meanings: "to pierce/bore/puncture" (its primary physical sense), "to specify/designate by name," and — in this unique context — "to blaspheme/profane by uttering"; the verb occurs also at v.16; the specific act is uttering the divine Name (ha-Shem — "the Name," the tetragrammaton YHWH) in the context of a curse, thereby "piercing" or profaning its sanctity; later Jewish tradition would be so sensitive about this that the divine Name was not pronounced at all] and he cursed [va-yeqallel — וַיְקַלֵּל; the Pi'el of q-l-l, "to curse/to make light/to treat as contemptible"; the opposite of k-b-d, "to honor/make heavy/give weight to"; one honors parents and God; to curse is to dishonor, to deny the weight and significance of the one cursed]; and they brought him [va-yavi'u oto — וַיָּבִיאוּ אֹתוֹ; the community immediately responds by bringing him to the appropriate authority] to Moshe [el-Moshe — אֶל-מֹשֶׁה; Moshe is the judicial authority for unprecedented cases, as throughout Exodus and Numbers], and his mother's name [ve-shem immo — וְשֵׁם אִמּוֹ] was Shelomit [Shelomit — שְׁלֹמִית; from sh-l-m, "peace/wholeness" — the one named woman in Lev; the naming of the mother (but not the father) is consistent with the narrative's emphasis on the Israelite maternal lineage], daughter of Divri [bat Divri — בַּת דִּבְרִי; Divri — perhaps from d-b-r, "word/speech"; a name dense with irony given that her son's sin was in the realm of speech], of the tribe of Dan [le-matteh Dan — לְמַטֵּה דָן; matteh — "staff/rod/tribe," one of the twelve tribes of Yisra'el; Dan is the tribe of the mother, establishing the Israelite lineage through which the son would claim membership in the community].

**24:12** And they placed him under guard [va-yannichhu oto ba-mishmar — וַיַּנִּיחֻהוּ בַּמִּשְׁמָר; mishmar — "a place of guarding/a watch/custody," from sh-m-r, "to watch/keep/guard"; the community places the man in custody pending a divine ruling — a procedural pattern also found in Num 15:34 and Lev 24:23; he is held, not summarily executed] to make a decision [lifros lahem — לִפְרֹשׁ לָהֶם; the Qal infinitive of p-r-sh, "to separate/explain/make distinct/make clear"; the phrase "to make clear/explain for them" — the community needs clarification on what the law requires] according to the mouth of the LORD [al-pi YHWH — עַל-פִּי יְהוָה; "according to the mouth of the LORD" — divine revelation through Moshe is required because the case is unprecedented: it has no prior ruling in the law already given].

**24:13** And the LORD spoke to Moshe, saying:

**24:14** Bring out the one who cursed [hotse et-ha-meqallel — הוֹצֵא אֶת-הַמְקַלֵּל; hotse — the Hifil imperative of y-ts-', "to bring out/cause to go out"; ha-meqallel — "the curser," a substantival Piel participle of q-l-l; note that the divine verdict uses the term for cursing (qillel) rather than piercing/blaspheming the Name (naqav), though both acts are condemned; the instruction to "bring out" the condemned person outside the camp before execution is a standard execution procedure, preserving the holiness of the camp — cf. Lev 4:12, Num 15:35] to the outside of the camp [el-mi-chutz la-machaneh — אֶל-מִחוּץ לַמַּחֲנֶה]; and all those who heard [ve-samkhu khol-ha-shomim et-yadeihem — וְסָמְכוּ כָל-הַשֹּׁמְעִים אֶת-יְדֵיהֶם; the witnesses — those who heard the blasphemy — lay their hands on his head before execution; the hand-laying (semikhah) by the witnesses is a ritual act that transfers responsibility and identifies the guilty party; cf. Lev 1:4 where semikhah identifies the sacrifice] shall lay their hands upon his head [al-rosho — עַל-רֹאשׁוֹ], and the entire congregation [ve-ragmu oto kol-ha-edah — וְרָגְמוּ אֹתוֹ כָּל-הָעֵדָה; kol-ha-edah — "all the congregation/assembly," the full covenant community as a corporate body; edah — from '-w-d, "to appoint/gather/testify"; the assembly that witnesses and executes] shall stone him [ragam — r-g-m, "to stone/hurl stones at"].

**24:15** And to the sons of Yisra'el you shall speak [ve-el-benei Yisra'el tedabber — וְאֶל-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל תְּדַבֵּר; the general law, abstracted from the specific case, is now articulated for the community], saying: Any man [ish ish — אִישׁ אִישׁ; the doubled "man man" is a Hebrew idiom of universality — "any man whatsoever," "whoever"; the same construction appears in the law of illicit sexual relations in Lev 18, 20] who curses his God [ki yeqallel Elohav — כִּי יְקַלֵּל אֱלֹהָיו; the verb is again q-l-l; "his God" — Elohav; the use of the more general Elohim rather than the tetragrammaton is notable; cursing God — the general category] shall bear his sin [ve-nasa chet'o — וְנָשָׂא חֶטְאוֹ; "shall carry/bear his sin/guilt" — the sin remains on him; the formula implies ongoing guilt and its consequences, including the ultimate divine judgment].

**24:16** And he who pierces the Name of the LORD [ve-noqev Shem YHWH — וְנֹקֵב שֵׁם יְהוָה; the Qal participle of n-q-v — "the one who pierces/blasphemes" — the specific act of v.11 now given as the general law; the divine Name — Shem YHWH — is specified, making this a more serious offense than the general cursing of God in v.15; the two verses thus form a graded scale: cursing God (v.15) / blaspheming the Name specifically (v.16)] shall surely be put to death [mot yumat — מוֹת יוּמַת; the Qal infinitive absolute + Hophal imperfect of m-w-t — the emphatic death formula "dying he shall be put to death"; the intensification via the doubling of the root is the standard formula for capital offenses in the Torah]; all the congregation [kol-ha-edah — כָּל-הָעֵדָה] shall surely stone him [ragom yirgemu vo — רָגוֹם יִרְגְּמוּ-בוֹ; again the infinitive absolute + imperfect construction for emphasis — "stoning they shall stone him"]; the sojourner as well as the native [ka-ger ka-ezrach — כַּגֵּר כָּאֶזְרָח; the law applies identically to both: ka (the preposition "as/like") before each term; ger — the sojourner/resident alien; ezrach — the native-born, from z-r-ch, "to rise/shine," the homeborn who "springs up" from the soil; this equivalence is the chapter's central theological assertion]: when he blasphemes the Name [be-noqvo et-ha-Shem — בְּנָקְבוֹ אֶת-הַשֵּׁם; the infinitive construct with preposition — "in his blaspheming the Name"] he shall be put to death [yumat — יוּמַת].

**24:17** And a man [ve-ish — וְאִישׁ] who strikes fatally any person [ki yakkeh kol-nefesh adam — כִּי יַכֶּה כָּל-נֶפֶשׁ אָדָם; ki yakkeh — "when he strikes"; kol-nefesh adam — "any person/soul of a human being"; nefesh adam — "the life of a human being," the living person; adam — a generic term for humanity as a kind; the striking (n-k-h, Hifil) that results in death] shall surely be put to death [mot yumat — מוֹת יוּמַת].

**24:18** And the one who strikes fatally an animal [u-makke nefesh-behemah — וּמַכֵּה נֶפֶשׁ-בְּהֵמָה; nefesh-behemah — "the life/soul of an animal"; behemah — a domestic animal, especially a large livestock animal; the lex talionis framework is now applied to property as well as persons] shall make restitution for it [yeshallemennah — יְשַׁלְּמֶנָּה; the Pi'el of sh-l-m, "to make whole/complete/pay back/make restitution"; the verb shillem is the practical financial form of shalom — to restore wholeness through compensatory payment] — life for life [nefesh tachat nefesh — נֶפֶשׁ תַּחַת נָפֶשׁ; the lex talionis formula in its most direct form: tachat — "in place of/under/instead of"; "life in place of life"; for animals this means financial restitution; the same formula applied to humans in v.20 means actual equivalence].

**24:19** And if a man inflicts a blemish [ve-ish ki yitten mum — וְאִישׁ כִּי-יִתֵּן מוּם; yitten — from n-t-n, "to give/inflict"; mum — "blemish/defect/flaw," the same term used for physical defects that disqualify animals from sacrifice and priests from service (Lev 21:17–21); to inflict a mum on a person is to damage them physically, creating in them a defect] in his fellow [ba-amito — בַּעֲמִיתוֹ; amito — "his fellow/his associate/his countryman," from '-m-t, related to the sense of community membership; see Lev 5:21; 18:20; 19:11, 15, 17], as he did [ka'asher asah — כַּאֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה; "just as he did" — the measure of the required response is the measure of the injury inflicted], so shall it be done to him [ken ye'aseh lo — כֵּן יֵעָשֶׂה לּוֹ; the passive — "thus shall be done to him" — leaving open whether this means literal physical retaliation or commensurate legal penalty].

**24:20** Fracture for fracture [shever tachat shaver — שֶׁבֶר תַּחַת שָׁבֶר; shever — "fracture/break," from sh-b-r; the lex talionis applied to bone-breaking], eye for eye [ayin tachat ayin — עַיִן תַּחַת עָיִן; the most famous of the talionic formulas (cf. Exod 21:24; Deut 19:21); ayin — "eye"; tachat — "in place of"], tooth for tooth [shen tachat shen — שֵׁן תַּחַת שֵׁן]; as he inflicted a blemish upon a person [ka'asher yitten mum ba-adam — כַּאֲשֶׁר יִתֵּן מוּם בָּאָדָם], so shall it be inflicted upon him [ken yinnaten bo — כֵּן יִנָּתֵן בּוֹ; the same passive construction as v.19 — the community or court enacts the equivalent penalty].

**24:21** And he who strikes an animal shall make restitution for it [u-makke behemah yeshallemennah — וּמַכֵּה בְהֵמָה יְשַׁלְּמֶנָּה; this verse summarizes the two categories in deliberately brief, parallel form], and he who strikes a person shall be put to death [u-makke adam yumat — וּמַכֵּה אָדָם יוּמָת; the contrast is stark and is the hinge of the entire lex talionis section: animals — restitution; persons — death; the asymmetry reflects the unique sanctity of the human person (cf. Gen 9:6)].

**24:22** One law [mishpat echad — מִשְׁפָּט אֶחָד; mishpat — "judgment/justice/law/ruling/custom," from sh-p-t, "to judge"; echad — "one/single/unified"; this is the chapter's great theological declaration — the covenant community operates under a single, undivided legal standard] you shall have [yihyeh lakhem — יִהְיֶה לָכֶם; "shall be for you" — it belongs to you, it is your possession and your commitment] for the sojourner [la-ger — לַגֵּר] and for the native [ve-la-ezrach — וְלָאֶזְרָח; both categories receive exactly the same legal treatment], for I am the LORD your God [ki ani YHWH Eloheikem — כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם; the self-identification formula that grounds the entire legal framework in the character of the LORD himself — one God, therefore one standard of justice].

**24:23** And Moshe spoke to the sons of Yisra'el [va-yedabber Moshe el-benei Yisra'el — וַיְדַבֵּר מֹשֶׁה אֶל-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל], and they brought out [va-yotsi'u — וַיּוֹצִיאוּ; the Hifil of y-ts-', "they brought out/caused to go forth"; carrying out the divine command of v.14] the one who had cursed [et-ha-meqallel — אֶת-הַמְקַלֵּל] to the outside of the camp [el-mi-chutz la-machaneh — אֶל-מִחוּץ לַמַּחֲנֶה], and they stoned him with stones [va-yirgemu oto even — וַיִּרְגְּמוּ אֹתוֹ אָבֶן; even — "stone," used here as the instrument-accusative; the action corresponds exactly to the divine command]; and the sons of Yisra'el did [va-ya'asu benei Yisra'el — וַיַּעֲשׂוּ בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל] as the LORD commanded Moshe [ka'asher tsivvah YHWH et-Moshe — כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת-מֹשֶׁה; the closing execution-report formula — exact obedience to the divine word; the chapter that opened with tsav (v.2), "command," closes with tsivvah — the LORD commanded and Yisra'el obeyed].

---

## Translator's Notes — EMET

### I. Structure and Integrity of the Chapter

Leviticus 24 combines two distinct literary forms — ritual prescription and narrative — within a single chapter, a phenomenon occurring elsewhere in Leviticus only at chapters 8–10 (narrative of Aaron's ordination and Nadab and Abihu's death) and chapter 16 (interweaving of the Yom Kippur ritual with narrative reference). The structure is:

- **vv.1–4:** The menorah oil law — the perpetual lamp
- **vv.5–9:** The lechem ha-panim law — the bread of the Presence
- **vv.10–16:** The narrative of the blasphemer, generating the law of blasphemy
- **vv.17–22:** The lex talionis interpolation — the general law occasioned by the case
- **v.23:** The execution of the sentence — narrative conclusion

The ritual legislation (vv.1–9) concerns the regular, perpetual maintenance of the sanctuary's light and its covenant-bread — both functions performed by the priests and funded by the community. The narrative (vv.10–23) concerns a singular, unrepeatable event that generates permanent, universal law. The two halves of the chapter are thus related thematically: both concern the maintenance of the LORD's sanctuary and community — one through ordered ritual, one through the enforcement of the boundaries of holy speech.

### II. Key Terms

**shemen zayit zakh katit (שֶׁמֶן זַיִת זַךְ כָּתִית, v.2):** "pure beaten olive oil." Three qualifiers: shemen zayit (olive oil, as opposed to other oils), zakh (pure/clear — only the limpid first flow, without sediment), katit (beaten — produced by pounding rather than pressing, ensuring purity). This is the highest quality olive oil available and was specified also in Exod 27:20 for the same purpose.

**ha-menorah ha-tehorah (הַמְּנֹרָה הַטְּהֹרָה, v.4):** "the pure/clean lampstand." The epithet tehorah (feminine of tahor) marks the menorah's ritual purity and its material — pure (tahor) gold. The menorah's seven branches and their theological significance (creation, divine wisdom, the seven days of the week) are here implied by its permanent role in illuminating the sanctuary.

**lechem ha-panim (לֶחֶם הַפָּנִים, implied throughout vv.5–9):** "bread of the Face/Presence." The term lechem ha-panim appears in Exod 25:30; Num 4:7; 1 Sam 21:7. In this passage the bread is referred to as lechem, challot, and its designation lechem ha-panim is implicit in the phrase lifnei YHWH (before the LORD/Face of the LORD). The twelve loaves represent the twelve tribes in the LORD's ongoing presence; their weekly replacement on the Sabbath enacts the renewal of the covenant.

**berit olam (בְּרִית עוֹלָם, v.8):** "an everlasting covenant." The bread of the Presence is the only sanctuary item in Leviticus explicitly designated a berit olam. This terminology — used elsewhere for the Noahic covenant (Gen 9:16), the Abrahamic covenant (Gen 17:7, 13, 19), and the Sabbath covenant (Exod 31:16) — elevates the weekly bread-offering to covenantal status. The implication is that the regular, reliable provision of bread before the LORD's face enacts and renews the covenant relationship between the LORD and Yisra'el.

**va-yiqqov et-ha-Shem (וַיִּקֹּב אֶת-הַשֵּׁם, v.11):** The verb n-q-v has a primary physical meaning of "to pierce/bore through." When applied to the divine Name, it carries the sense of "to utter distinctly/to pierce by naming" — to invoke the Name in a context that violates its sanctity. The later rabbinic practice of not pronouncing the tetragrammaton and substituting ha-Shem ("the Name") or Adonai derives partly from the extreme gravity attached to this act. In the narrative context, the "piercing" of the Name is associated with cursing (qillel), making the act a specific form of desecration — using the divine Name as a weapon in a human quarrel.

**ka-ger ka-ezrach (כַּגֵּר כָּאֶזְרָח, v.16):** "as the sojourner, so the native." The ger (sojourner/resident alien) and the ezrach (native-born) are the two fundamental social-legal categories in ancient Israelite law. Leviticus consistently extends legal obligations to both (cf. Lev 16:29; 17:8, 10, 12, 13, 15; 18:26; 19:33–34; 20:2; 22:18), and Lev 19:34 commands love of the sojourner. But the equivalence stated here — one law for both in the matter of blasphemy — is one of the most radical formulations, because capital law is applied without distinction of origin.

**mishpat echad (מִשְׁפָּט אֶחָד, v.22):** "one law/one judgment." The culminating declaration of the chapter. Mishpat echad is not merely procedural uniformity; it is a theological statement about the character of the LORD (who is echad, "one" — cf. Deut 6:4, the Shema) and the community he has created. The singularity (echad) of the LORD grounds the singularity (echad) of the law. There cannot be a different standard for insiders and outsiders in a community defined by the one God.

### III. The Lex Talionis (vv.17–22)

The lex talionis (Latin: "law of the like") in vv.17–22 is one of three occurrences in the Torah (cf. Exod 21:22–25; Deut 19:19–21). The principle — that punishment must be equivalent to injury — functions in all three contexts as a principle of proportionality limiting punishment, not (in its ancient Near Eastern context) requiring literal physical retaliation in every case. Ancient Near Eastern parallels (Code of Hammurabi §§196–201) show that monetary compensation was the standard practical application. The interpretation of the formula — literal vs. commensurate — was a major point of debate between Sadducees (literal) and Pharisees (monetary equivalent) in the Second Temple period. The rabbinic halakha followed the Pharisaic interpretation. The EMET translation renders the formulae literally, as the Hebrew text demands, without adjudicating the interpretive debate.

### IV. LXX Divergences (Vaticanus pp. 110–111)

The LXX (reflected in Vaticanus) translates v.11's va-yiqqov with ἐπονομάσας (eponomasas — "having named/invoked"), representing a specific Greek interpretive tradition: the blasphemer committed his offense by invoking (naming) the Name in a curse. The Hebrew verb naqav (pierce/specify) is elsewhere rendered in the LXX by trypan (to pierce), but in this specific blasphemy context the LXX chooses the verb meaning "to name," making the act definitively one of utterance. This LXX rendering would influence later discussion of what precisely constitutes blasphemy.

The LXX v.16 uses καταράσηται (katarasētai — "curses") for the second instance of naqav, apparently conflating the two verbs naqav and qillel into a single Greek word — a simplification that loses the Hebrew distinction between piercing/uttering the Name specifically (naqav) and the more general cursing (qillel).

### V. Canonical Connections

- **Exod 20:7 / Deut 5:11:** The prohibition of carrying/bearing (nasa') the Name of the LORD in vain — the foundational commandment of which Lev 24:10–16 is the enacted case-law.
- **Exod 27:20–21:** The parallel lamp-oil command, establishing the same tamid requirement.
- **Num 15:32–36:** A structurally parallel narrative — the Sabbath wood-gatherer — with the same pattern of custody (mishmar), divine ruling, and communal stoning; the two cases together illustrate the legal-procedural pattern for unprecedented capital cases in the wilderness.
- **Gen 9:6:** "Whoever sheds the blood of a human being, by a human being shall his blood be shed; for in the image of God (be-tselem Elohim) he made the human being" — the foundational rationale for human life's unique value implicit in the makke adam yumat of v.21.
